questions.prompt: What makes Next.js render a route dynamically (vs statically)?

choices.text: Any of: using `cookies()`, `headers()`, or `searchParams` (request-time data); using `fetch()` with `cache: 'no-store'`; explicitly declaring `export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic'`. Without these, routes render statically at build time and serve from CDN. Add `cookies()` for auth and the page becomes dynamic automatically. This is why marketing pages stay fast (no opt-in needed) while dashboards naturally become dynamic just by virtue of reading the session cookie.

questions.explanation: Dynamic functions (cookies/headers/searchParams) or no-store fetches force dynamic.

questions.prompt: Blog with 5,000 posts that update occasionally. Best rendering strategy?

choices.text: ISR: `generateStaticParams()` returns all post slugs (pre-rendered at build); `export const revalidate = 3600` re-generates each post at most every hour. With `dynamicParams = true` (default), new posts render on-demand at first hit and cache. Result: existing posts served from CDN at ~50ms; updates propagate within an hour without rebuilding; new posts work immediately. Best of static (fast) + dynamic (fresh-enough) for content-heavy sites that don't need real-time updates.

questions.explanation: ISR: generateStaticParams + revalidate window. Fast + fresh-enough.
